notes.note: The fortified harbour town some five miles southwest of Athens, the city's port since the fifth century BC; Cicero landed here on his homeward voyage and received Atticus's letter the moment he stepped ashore.

notes.anchor: the twelfth day before the Kalends of October
notes.note: Reckoned inclusively in the Roman manner, this is 20 September 50 BC, the day Atticus reached Rome with a fever; the inclusive count makes it twelve, not ten, days before the Kalends (the first) of October.

The two censors of 50 BC, App. Claudius Pulcher and L. Calpurnius Piso, conducted the census and revised the Senate roll; Cicero is anxious about their high-handed measures, including a proposed sumptuary crackdown on luxury art.

Signa et tabulae, statues and panel-paintings, were the prized luxury goods of wealthy Roman houses; Cicero wonders whether the censors will bring forward a measure restricting their ownership, a matter touching his own collection.

notes.anchor: Caesar reaches Placentia with four legions
notes.note: Placentia (modern Piacenza) on the Po was a key town of Cisalpine Gaul; Caesar's moving four legions there in October 50 BC brought his army to the edge of Italy, an ominous prelude to the crossing of the Rubicon in January 49.

The arx of Athens is the Acropolis; Cicero plays on the military sense of statio ('guard-post'), wryly casting his stop at Athens as the sentry-station from which he watches the gathering storm at Rome.
